content.full_text:
RAVI MEHTA
CHAIRMAN

FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION

April 14, 1997

Ms. Renee Mueller
City of Berkeley
Martin Luther King, Jr.
Civic Center Building
2180 Milvia Street
Berkeley, California 94704

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-97-149

Dear Ms. Mueller:

This letter is a response to your request for informal assistance regarding the provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ We are treating your letter as a request for informal assistance because you have not identified the specific officials on whose behalf you are seeking advice. Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with immunity. (Seismic 83114(b); Regulation 18329.)

QUESTIONS

1. May a salaried city employee be the treasurer for an officeholder account and receive his or her usual salary for the performance of those duties?

2. May a salaried city employee fundraise for an officeholder account and receive his or her usual salary for performance of those duties?

3. If questions number 1 and 2 are answered affirmatively, does the salary constitute a contribution to the officeholder account thereby limiting it to $250 per calendar year pursuant to Section 85313(b) of the Government Code?

___________________

¹ Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109 - 18995, of the California Code of Regulations.

428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814 (916) 322-5660

File No. A-97-149
Page 2

CONCLUSIONS

1. Yes. There is nothing in the Act prohibiting a salaried city employee from being
treasurer for an officeholder account.

2. Yes. There is nothing in the Act prohibiting a salaried city employee from raising
funds for an officeholder account.

3. Yes. If a city employee receives his or her salary for raising funds or performing
treasurer duties, the salary will constitute a contribution to the officeholder account and will be
limited to $250 per calendar year.

ANALYSIS

Section 85313(a) permits each elected officer to establish an officeholder expense fund
"for expenses related to assisting, serving, or communicating with constituents, or with carrying
out the official duties of the elected officer." No contribution to the officeholder account may
exceed $250 and aggregate contributions to the account may not exceed $10,000 per year.
(Section 85313.) Section 85313 goes on to state that "[c]ontributions to an officeholder account
shall not be considered campaign contributions." All contributions are, however, subject to the
campaign disclosure and reporting requirements. (Section 85313(d).)

You have inquired as to whether city employees may use city time to raise officeholder
funds for city elected officials or to act as treasurer for a city official's officeholder account. As
you know, the Commission administers and enforces the Political Reform Act. The Commission
does not regulate other state statutes regarding the misuse of public funds or local rules
governing the misuse of public funds. Therefore, this advice is limited to the provisions of the
Act.

You specifically inquired as to whether this activity would implicate Section 85300.
Section 85300 provides that no public officer shall expend and no candidate shall accept any
public moneys for the purpose of seeking elective office. Under your facts, the city employees
would be raising funds for officeholder accounts, not for campaign accounts. Since this activity
will not be campaign activity or intended to influence the action of the voters for or against the
nomination or election of a candidate or candidates, it will not implicate Section 85300.

The next issue is whether city employees' time spent working on officeholder accounts
would be considered contributions to the officeholder accounts. The general term "contribution"
is defined in the Act to include volunteer personal services if the volunteer performs the services
with an understanding or agreement that he or she will be compensated for the services. (Section
82015.) Such services are considered nonmonetary contributions if, as in this case, employees
are reimbursed by their employers for performing personal services for candidates.

File No. A-97-149
Page 3

Regulation 18420 provides that the payment by a state or local government agency of the
salary or expenses of its employees or agents is a contribution only if the employee spends more
than 10 percent of his or her time in a calendar month on campaign activities specifically defined
by the regulation. (Regulation 18420.) These campaign activities include fundraising and
preparing campaign statements. (Regulation 18420(b).)

While the Commission has an extensive regulatory definition of campaign contributions,
it has not yet defined contributions with regard to officeholder accounts. (Regulation 18215.)
The definition of contribution in Regulation 18215 does not necessarily translate well to
officeholder accounts, and the Commission will be reviewing the language of Sections 85313
and 82015 to determine the best definition for officeholder funds. (See Gallegos Advice Letter,
No. A-97-052.) In addition, Regulation 18420 was passed before the advent of officeholder
accounts, as defined by Section 85313. Thus, Regulation 18420 does not apply directly to city
employees' activities on behalf of officeholder accounts.

The reasoning behind Regulation 18420 is instructive on this point, however.
Officeholder accounts are funds held by the officeholder in a committee used for the purpose of
assisting and communicating with constituents, as well as carrying out the officer's official
duties. They are not public funds or political contributions, but rather donations to the
officeholder from supporters. As noted above, pursuant to the statutory definition of
contribution, services provided by employees who are reimbursed by their employers are
considered nonmonetary contributions. Officeholders may receive nonmonetary contributions
into their officeholder accounts, but such contributions are limited to $250. (Ackerman Advice
Letter, No. A-97-065.)

Regulation 18420 permits city employees to support their employers on policy or
constituent services without their salary payments being considered contributions. The
regulation draws the line, however, at overt political activity, including raising funds or setting
campaign strategy. While raising money for officeholder funds would not be considered political
activity, it is also not policy assistance or service to constituents. Although the funds will
eventually be used for communicating with or assisting constituents, the fundraising and
accounting services will be provided directly to the officeholder. Therefore, following the model
of Regulation 18420, we conclude that if a city employee spends more than 10 percent of his or

File No. A-97-149
Page 4

her time to raise funds or acts as a treasurer for an elected official's officeholder account and
receives his or her usual salary for those services, the salary constitutes a contribution to the
officeholder account that may not exceed $250. (Section 85313.)

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-
5660.

Sincerely,

Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel

By: Liane Randolph
Staff Counsel, Legal Division

SGC:LR:ak

limited to $250 per calendar year.
